content.value: A decoder converts n binary input lines into up to 2^n unique output lines, activating exactly one output for each input combination. A 3-to-8 decoder, for example, has 3 inputs and 8 outputs, with one output line going high (or low, for active-low decoders) per input code.
Decoders are commonly used for memory address decoding - selecting exactly one memory chip or register based on an address.

An encoder is the opposite of a decoder: it converts one active input line (out of 2^n) back into an n-bit binary code. A basic encoder assumes only one input is active at a time; a priority encoder resolves the case where multiple inputs are active by outputting the code for the highest-priority input.
A multiplexer selects one of several data inputs and routes it to a single output, based on a set of select lines. A 4-to-1 MUX needs 2 select lines (2^2 = 4 inputs).

4-to-1 MUX select logic:
S1 S0 | Output
0  0  |  I0
0  1  |  I1
1  0  |  I2
1  1  |  I3

A demultiplexer takes a single input and routes it to one of several outputs based on select lines - functionally the reverse of a multiplexer. A demultiplexer with select lines is structurally identical to a decoder with an added enable input.

Any Boolean function of n variables can be implemented with a 2^(n-1)-to-1 MUX: place n-1 variables on the select lines, and wire each data input to 0, 1, the remaining variable, or its complement, based on the truth table rows for that select combination.
When asked to implement a function using a MUX in an exam, build a quick truth table first, group rows by the select-line variables, and read off what each data input needs to be.

quiz.question: How many outputs does a 3-to-8 decoder have?
What does a multiplexer do?
options: Routes one of several inputs to a single output based on select lines
Converts an active line into binary code
Always doubles the number of inputs

quiz.question: A priority encoder is needed when:
options: Only one input is ever active
Multiple inputs can be active simultaneously and one must be chosen
